evidence_quotes.quote: So-called dark patterns or deliberately manipulative marketing must therefore be strictly rejected at all times.
The game only works as long as the publisher provides a server that the game contacts via the internet when it starts up and tells the game that it should/may start.
Publishers shut down these servers: sometimes after ten years, sometimes after only two years, sometimes after just a few months.
The result: the game no longer starts, even though users have installed the game completely with all game data on their own computers.
Customers are therefore forced to buy a new game and spend money because they can no longer play their old favourite game.
Please set a precedent here and require publishers by law to ensure that, after the end of the service period, a game remains usable/playable for purchasers.

This does not mean that publishers will incur costs or have to keep their servers running permanently. It means that publishers must modify the game at the end of the service period so that no server and no internet connection is required.
If the purchaser has installed the game on their own computer, they must be able to start and play it offline.

paragraphs.text: Dear Sir or Madam, I fully support your initiative for the Digital Fairness Act. Consumers must always have complete and total control over how they spend and use their money digitally. So-called dark patterns or deliberately manipulative marketing must therefore be strictly rejected at all times. The principle applies: what is prohibited offline must also be prohibited online. To this end, it must be ensured that things that can be restricted offline must also be restricted online. I therefore ask of you to extend the Digital Fairness Act to include entertainment media, in particular video games. The reason: In recent years, it has become standard practice for major publishers the companies that release and market video games to make their games online-only. The game only works as long as the publisher provides a server that the game contacts via the internet when it starts up and tells the game that it should/may start. These publishers are currently shutting down many of those servers: sometimes after ten years, sometimes after only two years, sometimes after just a few months. The result: the game no longer starts, even though users have installed the game completely with all game data on their own computers. Background: Publishers shut down these servers so that players buy a new game again and don't keep playing the old one. Customers are therefore forced to buy a new game and spend money because they can no longer play their old favourite game. This means that publishers earn even more money than they already do. The video game industry is the largest entertainment media industry in the world and generates more revenue worldwide each year than the entire film, TV and music industries combined. Please apply this concept to the offline world. You purchase a new car, for example, an Audi. You drive this car for six years, are extremely satisfied, and the car has no issues even after six years. Everything still functions: the engine, transmission, electronics, nothing is broken. The next day, you wake up and Audi has modified your car via a software update so that it no longer starts. Not at all. No matter what you try. You complain to Audi and Audi informs you that Our service period for this car has now expired and If you want to continue driving an Audi, you will have to buy a new Audi. At the same time, you are offered the latest model for purchase. Sounds absurd? Yes, it is. Such a practice would never be permitted in the offline world. However, this has been happening in the video game industry for years, and no one is doing anything about it. Please set a precedent here and require publishers by law to ensure that, after the end of the service period, a game remains usable/playable for purchasers. This does not mean that publishers will incur costs or have to keep their servers running permanently. It means that publishers must modify the game at the end of the service period so that no server and no internet connection is required. If the purchaser has installed the game on their own computer, they must be able to start and play it offline. It's that simple. As simple as inserting a key into a car lock and driving off. Thank you very much!
